waru-pass-trek.title: Palampur Drive to Chandpur and Trek to Rajol Camp
waru-pass-trek.trailType: jeep track turning to footpath, riverside trail along the Awe Khad
waru-pass-trek.description: The Waru Pass crossing begins with a drive out of Palampur, the road unwinding through Kangra's dense tea gardens and terraced fields before climbing toward Chandpur, a small roadhead village where jeep tracks finally surrender to footpath. From here the group shoulders daypacks and drops onto a broad trail running close to the Awe Khad, the stream that will accompany much of the route toward Satchali over the coming days, its water still carrying the turbid brown tint of lower catchment silt rather than the clear glacial colour it takes on higher up. Terraced fields of maize and rajma give way within the first hour to mixed forest of chir pine, oak, and rhododendron, the canopy thickening as the trail gains height above the last permanent Gaddi hamlets on this stretch. Cowbells and the occasional bark of a guard dog drift up from scattered farmsteads still visible below, a last, faint reminder of settled valley life before the walk properly turns wild. The going today is deliberately gentle, following old grazing paths worn smooth by generations of shepherd families moving flocks between Kangra and Chamba each summer, and the modest distance covered is designed to let bodies settle into rhythm rather than push pace. Rajol, the day's camp, is a flattened forest clearing beside the Awe Khad, shaded by tall deodar and used seasonally by Gaddi herders as a halting ground on their own migration. Tents go up on soft, needle-covered ground within earshot of the stream, and guides use the relaxed afternoon to check gear, redistribute loads, and talk through the days ahead, including how remote and lightly trekked this particular crossing remains compared to better-known Dhauladhar passes. As evening settles, mist often drifts up from the valley floor and collects in the trees, and dinner is eaten to the steady, unbroken sound of the Awe Khad below camp, a gentle first night before the forest climb properly begins tomorrow.
highlights: Drive through Kangra's tea gardens to the Chandpur roadhead
First walk beside the Awe Khad stream
Passing the last permanent Gaddi hamlets on the route
Sheltered deodar-forest camp at Rajol
tips: Keep the first day's pace deliberately slow, this trek gains height steadily over the following week
Carry a few layers even on this lower stretch, mist rolls in quickly once the sun drops
Fill water bottles at Rajol before dark, the streamside site is the last easy access point before higher, colder camps

waru-pass-trek.title: Rajol to Kandi Got through Deodar Forest
waru-pass-trek.trailType: steep forest switchbacks, deodar and horse chestnut cover, scattered shepherd clearings
waru-pass-trek.description: Leaving Rajol, the trail commits properly to the forest, climbing in long, steady switchbacks through stands of deodar and horse chestnut that block out much of the sky and keep the morning noticeably cool even once the sun is well up. The Awe Khad drops further below with every switchback gained, its sound fading to a distant murmur, while the character of the forest shifts gradually from broadleaf to a darker, denser mix of conifer as elevation increases. Occasional clearings interrupt the climb, small sunlit meadows used by shepherds as brief grazing halts, where scattered stone rings mark old fire pits and moss-covered rock shelters offer a glimpse of how thoroughly this route has been worked into the seasonal rhythm of Gaddi pastoral life for generations. Birdlife is active through the middle stretches, with monal pheasant occasionally startled from the undergrowth and treepie calls carrying through the canopy, while pika and the odd langur troop are spotted moving through the higher branches. The trail steepens noticeably in the final hour before Kandi Got, the forest thinning as the group climbs above the densest belt of tree cover and into a mixed zone of stunted conifer and open grass. Kandi Got itself is a broad, sloping meadow clearing wedged between forest and open hillside, chosen by generations of trekking parties and shepherds alike for its flat pitching ground and a dependable spring a short walk from camp. Views back down the valley toward Kangra's terraced lower slopes open up properly for the first time here, a wide green sweep receding toward the plains, while ahead the ridgeline hiding Satchali and the approach to Waru Pass itself becomes visible as a rough grey outline against the sky. The climb has been genuinely demanding, and most trekkers are glad of an early dinner and an unusually early night, legs and lungs both registering the day's steady altitude gain.
highlights: Long deodar and horse chestnut forest climb
Old shepherd clearings with stone fire rings
Monal pheasant and langur sightings through the forest
First distant view of the Waru Pass ridgeline
tips: This is one of the longest climbing days on the route, pace it steadily rather than in bursts
Carry enough water for the middle stretch, reliable sources thin out between forest sections
Watch footing on the steeper switchbacks near Kandi Got, loose leaf litter can hide slick rock underneath

waru-pass-trek.title: Kandi Got to Satchali, Meeting Point of Seven Rivulets
waru-pass-trek.trailType: thinning forest into open glacial flat, boulder-strewn streambanks
waru-pass-trek.description: Today's walk carries the group out of continuous forest for good, the trees thinning progressively into scattered birch and juniper before disappearing almost entirely as the trail crosses into Satchali's open glacial basin. The path follows the Awe Khad's upper reaches closely, the water here running a colder, clearer grey-blue than the silted lower stretches, fed increasingly by snowmelt rather than rain runoff. Boulder fields become a regular feature through the middle of the day, some old and lichen-covered, others freshly deposited by winter avalanche activity, and the group picks a careful line across them with poles doing much of the balancing work. Satchali takes its name, according to local Gaddi tradition, from the seven separate rivulets that converge here into a single glacial flat, each one draining a different fold of the ridge system feeding Waru Pass, and the sound of running water from multiple directions at once gives the camp a distinctly restless, watery character even at rest. The Satchali glacier itself, a modest but genuine ice field tucked into a north-facing hollow above camp, remains visible from the tent doors on clear afternoons, its pale surface streaked with rock debris carried down from the surrounding slopes. This is one of the loneliest and least visited campsites on the entire Dhauladhar range, well off the more familiar Indrahar and Minkiani corridors, and the total absence of any other trekking group is often remarked on by those who have walked other, busier passes in the region. Wildflowers, particularly primula and anemone, cling to sheltered pockets between the boulders through the peak summer weeks, a surprising splash of colour against otherwise bare grey rock and pale glacial silt. Guides use the relatively early arrival to point out the line the group will take toward Baoli Got and the pass beyond, and to brief everyone on the demanding, wind-exposed crossing still two days away. Dinner is taken with down jackets already on, the cold at this altitude settling in fast once the sun drops behind the ridge.
highlights: Reaching Satchali, the confluence of seven glacial rivulets
First close view of the Satchali glacier
Boulder-field walking beside the upper Awe Khad
One of the most lightly visited campsites in the Dhauladhar
tips: Pick your line across the boulder fields carefully, wet rock near the streambanks can be deceptively slick
Refill water at Satchali's confluence pools, the taste and clarity noticeably improve above this point
Keep cameras handy in the late afternoon, the glacier catches the best light just before sunset

waru-pass-trek.title: Rest and Acclimatisation Day at Satchali with a Walk to the Glacier Snout
waru-pass-trek.trailType: off-trail moraine walk along the glacier margin, boulder hopping
waru-pass-trek.description: With the pass still two days off and the body needing time to adjust to nearly 9,500 feet, today is kept deliberately light, built around rest rather than distance. The group sets out unladen on a short exploratory walk along the moraine ridge above camp, following a faint shepherd path toward the snout of the Satchali glacier itself rather than any formal trekking route. The going is rough and occasionally requires hands-on scrambling over loose, angular boulders, but the reward is a close, unhurried look at the ice, its lower face streaked grey-black with rock flour and studded with small meltwater pools that glow an improbable turquoise in direct sun. Standing this close to a working Himalayan glacier, away from any of the more visited ice fields further west in Lahaul or Spiti, brings home how genuinely remote and lightly documented this particular corner of the Dhauladhar remains. The return route drops back toward camp via a slightly different line, tracing the highest of Satchali's seven converging rivulets to its source in a scree-lined hollow, before rejoining the main streambed for the walk back. The remainder of the day is left free, and most trekkers use it to wash gear in the icy water, repair blisters, sort loads for the demanding stretch ahead, and simply sit with the sound of moving water and the sight of the glacier catching afternoon light. Guides run a detailed briefing on Waru Pass itself in the late afternoon, explaining its local reputation as a wind funnel and the importance of an early start on the crossing day, along with basic checks on everyone's acclimatisation and general condition. Marmots are frequently seen sunning themselves on flat rocks near camp through the warmer hours, entirely undisturbed by the unusual sight of visitors. A second night at the same altitude, without further gain, gives bodies real time to adjust, and the extra rest is felt clearly in the energy levels visible around the fire that evening.
highlights: Close approach to the Satchali glacier snout
Tracing one of the seven rivulets to its scree-lined source
Marmot sightings on the rocks around camp
A genuine rest day to acclimatise before the pass push
tips: Treat this as real rest, not a bonus trekking day, resist the urge to push further toward the glacier than the guides recommend
Sunscreen and glacier glasses matter here, glare off the ice is stronger than the temperature suggests
Use the free afternoon to dry out boots and socks fully before the higher, colder camps ahead

waru-pass-trek.title: Satchali to Baoli Got below the Final Headwall
waru-pass-trek.trailType: bare scree slope, hardened snow nullah crossings, narrowing headwall valley
waru-pass-trek.description: The trail out of Satchali climbs immediately and does not properly relent again until camp, marking a clear shift in character from the previous days' forest and moraine walking to something closer to true high-altitude terrain. Grass and scattered scrub give way within the first hour to bare scree and compacted snow nullahs, old avalanche runs that hold snow well into midsummer and require careful, kicked-step footing to cross safely. The valley narrows steadily into a proper headwall, rock walls closing in on both sides and funnelling an increasingly persistent wind down from the direction of the still-hidden pass, an early taste of the conditions Waru is locally famous for. Streams here run icy and fast, fed directly by snowmelt just above, and several crossings require a careful, cold-footed wade where no snow bridge holds. Baoli Got, the name referring to a natural spring found near the site, sits on a narrow rock and turf shelf wedged below the final headwall, one of the last flat pitching spots before the terrain turns to continuous scree leading up to the pass itself. The site feels exposed and elemental compared to every previous camp on this trek, ringed by bare grey rock rather than any grass or forest, and cloud frequently builds along the ridgeline above through the afternoon before clearing again toward evening. From camp the notch of Waru Pass becomes visible for the first time as a distinct saddle on the skyline, considerably lower and less jagged than the surrounding peaks, consistent with its local reputation as a wind gap rather than a true mountaineering col. Guides spend the evening running a detailed briefing on tomorrow's crossing, covering pacing, the importance of an early departure to avoid the wind picking up through the day, and the steep, glaciated nullah descent waiting on the Chamba side. Temperatures drop sharply after sunset, and most trekkers retreat to their tents earlier than usual, conscious that tomorrow is the trek's genuine test.
highlights: First direct sighting of the Waru Pass saddle
Crossing hardened snow nullahs below the headwall
Exposed high camp beside the Baoli Got spring
Detailed evening briefing ahead of the pass crossing
tips: Kick firm steps deliberately on the snow nullah crossings rather than sliding, the surface is harder than it looks
Pack your summit-day layers accessible tonight, mornings here start well below freezing
Secure tents thoroughly, this camp sits in a natural wind corridor even before the pass wind proper begins

waru-pass-trek.title: Baoli Got over Waru Pass, the Gateway of Wind, to Thanetar
waru-pass-trek.trailType: funnel-shaped windy pass, loose scree and old snow, steep glaciated nullah descent
waru-pass-trek.description: This is the day the entire trek has been building toward, and the group leaves Baoli Got in cold, pre-dawn light to reach the pass while the overnight freeze still holds the scree and snow firm underfoot. The climb is steep from the very first step, a rough line picked across loose rock and old, hardened snow nullahs, and breathing grows noticeably harder through the final few hundred feet as the trail narrows into the funnel-shaped defile that gives Waru its local name, the Gateway of Wind. Even on an otherwise calm morning, air accelerates through this constriction with real force, and the last approach to the saddle is done leaning slightly into a wind that seems to originate from nowhere and everywhere at once. Cresting Waru Pass at just over 12,600 feet delivers a genuinely wide panorama, back toward the layered green ridges of the Kangra valley and the distant haze of the plains, and ahead toward the tighter, more folded terrain of upper Chamba, still largely forested but noticeably steeper and more shadowed than the Kangra-facing slopes just crossed. There is no crowd here, no cairn worn smooth by heavy foot traffic, only loose rock and wind, a reminder of how far this particular crossing sits from the Dhauladhar's more frequented routes. The descent begins immediately and does not ease for hours, dropping through a long, glaciated nullah still holding old snow in its upper reaches before giving way to scree, then loose scrub, then the first proper forest of the Chamba side. Thanetar, the night's camp, sits in a partially forested hollow well below the pass, sheltered enough that the wind of the crossing feels like a different world entirely by the time tents go up. Legs are tired and knees sore from the long, technical descent, and dinner conversation returns repeatedly to the sheer force of wind felt at the saddle, each trekker comparing notes on the exact moment it hit hardest.
highlights: Crossing Waru Pass, the Gateway of Wind, at over 12,600 ft
Wide panorama spanning Kangra and upper Chamba
Long, technical descent through a glaciated nullah
Sheltered forest camp at Thanetar after the crossing
tips: Leave before first light, the pass's wind builds noticeably as the day warms
Trekking poles are essential on the descent, the loose scree causes more slips than the climb itself
Layer up specifically for the saddle itself, the wind chill there is far sharper than the surrounding slopes

waru-pass-trek.title: Thanetar to Nayagaon via the Kali Temple
waru-pass-trek.trailType: slushy forest path, short climb to a cliffside temple, long descent through mixed forest
waru-pass-trek.description: With the pass now well behind the group, today's walk trades yesterday's tension for a long, steady descent through increasingly settled Chamba-side terrain, the trail following an old shepherd and trade route that has linked this valley to Kangra for generations. The path is narrow and, in places, genuinely slippery, cutting across spring-fed ground and loose slate that demands careful footing even on the gentler stretches, a reminder that this side of the Dhauladhar sees noticeably different rainfall patterns than the Kangra approach. Around the midpoint of the day, a short, sharp climb leads to a small, weathered Kali temple perched on a rock outcrop above the trail, a modest structure of stone and wood that local Gaddi families still visit during seasonal migrations between the two valleys, and the group typically pauses here to rest and take in the view back toward the pass, now a distant notch on the skyline. Beyond the temple, the trail drops steadily through mixed forest that thickens noticeably as elevation is lost, deodar and oak giving way lower down to walnut and wild apricot trees marking the first signs of cultivated ground. Small hamlets begin to appear on the terraced slopes below, their slate roofs and timber-frame construction distinctly Chamba in style, quite different from the stone and tin architecture common on the Kangra side. Nayagaon, also known locally as Dhog, is reached by mid-to-late afternoon, a scattered village set among walnut orchards where the trek proper effectively concludes, road access finally returning after a week of walking. Camp is pitched on a grassy field at the village edge, and the sudden reappearance of everyday village life, barking dogs, children playing, smoke from cooking fires, feels almost startling after days of near-total wilderness. Guides use the evening to reflect on the crossing as a whole with the group, noting how rarely this particular route sees outside visitors compared to the well-known passes further west, and dinner carries a distinct sense of quiet accomplishment.
highlights: Cliffside Kali temple with views back toward Waru Pass
Transition into distinctly Chamba-style village architecture
Walnut and wild apricot orchards near Nayagaon
Return to road access after a week of remote walking
tips: Watch your footing closely on the slushy stretches below Thanetar, slate underfoot turns slick fast
Carry small change or sweets if you want to acknowledge villagers along the final approach
Save some water capacity for the midday climb to the temple, there is no reliable source right at the top

waru-pass-trek.title: Nayagaon to Dhog Roadhead and Drive to Bharmour, Trek Concludes
waru-pass-trek.trailType: village path, motorable dirt road down to the Dhog roadhead
waru-pass-trek.description: The final morning is short and unhurried, a last walk down from the Nayagaon camp along a broadening dirt track that soon becomes properly motorable, marking the formal end of the trekking stretch of the Waru Pass crossing. Villagers along the way, many recognisable from the previous evening's brief encounters, offer friendly nods and the occasional invitation for tea, a low-key send-off typical of this lightly visited corner of the Chamba valley. At the roadhead in Dhog, vehicles are waiting to carry the group onward, and the drive toward Bharmour winds through steadily thickening deodar forest and past small terraced fields before the valley opens into the wider bowl containing the town itself, historic capital of the old Chamba kingdom before the seat shifted to Chamba town centuries ago. Time is usually built in for a visit to the Chaurasi temple complex, a cluster of some eighty-four ancient shrines built in wood and stone, dedicated to a range of deities and standing as one of the most significant historic sites in the entire Dhauladhar-Chamba region, its carved doorways and shikhara-style towers a striking contrast to the plain stone huts and rock shelters seen throughout the trek. Walking among the temples, still actively used for worship by local Gaddi and Chamba communities, offers a fitting counterpoint to a week spent almost entirely in uninhabited high terrain, connecting the wild upper valleys just crossed to the settled cultural landscape they have always fed into. From Bharmour, onward road connections run toward Chamba town and further to Dalhousie or Pathankot, and the group typically disperses from here, carrying back stories of a crossing that, unlike the region's more famous passes, offered near-total solitude, a genuine glacier at Satchali, and one of the windiest, strangest little saddles in the entire Dhauladhar range.
highlights: Easy final walk down to the Dhog roadhead
Scenic drive into the historic town of Bharmour
Visit to the ancient eighty-four-shrine Chaurasi temple complex
Trek conclusion with onward connections toward Chamba and Dalhousie
tips: Keep footwear on for the temple visit, the courtyard stones can be uneven and occasionally slippery
Arrange onward transport from Bharmour a day ahead if heading straight to Pathankot, options thin out later in the day
Take time at the Chaurasi complex, it rewards a slow, unhurried walk rather than a quick photo stop
